title: Embed default-policy into binary
body:
This should make the usage of the skopeo statically linked binary a lot simpler.

- Refs https://github.com/containers/skopeo/issues/1648#issuecomment-1580905503

comments.body: I tested it, and it works nice.

The build errors doesn't seem related:

```
RPM build errors:
    source_date_epoch_from_changelog set but %changelog is missing
    File not found: /builddir/build/BUILDROOT/skopeo-1.12.1~dev-1.20230607224542237766.pr2014.36.gfb180f03.fc39.aarch64/usr/share/man/man1/%{name}*
Finish: rpmbuild skopeo-1.12.1~dev-1.20230607224542237766.pr2014.36.gfb180f03.fc39.src.rpm
```
How about hide this "feature" behind a compile flag, so that only users building the static binary can set it?
A static binary having a different security behavior from a dynamic one would be an even more risky footgun.

Also consider the supply chain security risks of individually distributed static binaries.

Distribution packages really are strongly recommended over that, for many reasons.

Alternatively there is https://github.com/containers/skopeo/tree/main/contrib/skopeoimage .

> Silently ignoring failures is not acceptable.

This is not silently ignoring all failures, only if no `policy.json` is found on the system. In this case, the user would most likely just install the default one anyway (by using `make install` for example). This just cuts this extra step.

> A static binary having a different security behavior from a dynamic one would be an even more risky footgun.

Considering that the dynamic one distributed through package managers also ships the same `policy.json` by default, I don't see much of a difference in this case.

> Also consider the supply chain security risks of individually distributed static binaries.
> 
> Distribution packages really are strongly recommended over that, for many reasons.
> 
> Alternatively there is https://github.com/containers/skopeo/tree/main/contrib/skopeoimage .

I understand, but this PR is not to argue whether static binaries are good or bad. I understand and respect your opinion as a maintainer to not encourage the usage or distribute static binaries by yourself.

This PR is just to make it simpler to get started with `skopeo` when building it from source, which is especially useful for statically linked binaries.

I'll also understand if you prefer to close this PR to avoid, perhaps, encouraging the use of static binaries.

My opinion on static binaries is not nearly as strong as my opinion that the security enforcing code needs to be paranoid about both bugs and human operators’ mistakes.

E.g. users could intend to ship a config file by something like Ansible, but make a typo in the path. Or a mount command could fail.

Of course users are free to patch their own systems, or to wrap Skopeo in a shell script that always adds `--insecure-policy`.

And ignoring signatures may well be the right call for some environments.

I just don’t think it’s the right trade-off for wide distribution.
